groups.name: Gap-Filling Canopy Oaks
groups.why: Fast, broadly adaptable canopy for all gap types. Pre-settlement forests were oak-dominated; modern restoration under-represents them. Oaks support more wildlife species than any other genus.
groups.mfNote: For Management-First: direct seed acorns from nearby legacy trees instead — free and locally adapted. Skip purchasing oaks.

species.name: Northern Red Oak
species.note: Fastest-growing oak (24"/yr). 500+ Lepidoptera species.

species.name: White Oak
species.note: Generational anchor. Best acorn quality. Deep burgundy fall color.

species.name: Black Oak
species.note: Ridge and slope companion. Highly adaptable to acidic upland soils.

species.name: Scarlet Oak
species.note: Dramatic scarlet fall color. Dry rocky companion to chestnut and black oak. 400+ Lepidoptera. Absorbs overflow.

groups.name: Moist-Slope Canopy
groups.why: For north-facing slopes where beech is dying. These species fill the beech niche. If maples are already regenerating naturally on your site, reduce Sugar Maple and Basswood absorbs.

species.name: Sugar Maple
species.note: Primary beech replacement. Wire cage required — tubes overheat this species.

species.name: Yellow Birch
species.note: Native at 1,800–2,500ft. Needs mineral soil to germinate. Wire cage.

species.name: Sweet Birch
species.note: Wintergreen-scented bark. Well-adapted to rocky Appalachian slopes throughout NEPA. Wire cage — heat-sensitive.

species.name: Basswood
species.note: #1 bee tree in eastern N. America. Absorbs overflow.

groups.name: Ridge & Dry-Slope Canopy
groups.why: Dry rocky ridges and upper slopes. Pre-settlement forests were oak-HICKORY co-dominant — modern restoration forgets the hickory half. These three belong together on the ridge.

species.name: Chestnut Oak
species.note: Ridge oak at your elevation. Management-First: auto-zeroed — hickories absorb this allocation.

species.name: Shagbark Hickory
species.note: Loose bark = critical bat roosting habitat. Pre-settlement dominant. Takes 50–70 yrs to bat-roosting size.

species.name: Pignut Hickory
species.note: Most appropriate hickory for upper ridges to 4,900ft.

species.name: Mockernut Hickory
species.note: Third hickory for dry upper ridges alongside pignut and shagbark — restores pre-settlement three-hickory co-dominance. Absorbs overflow.

groups.name: Pioneers / Fast-Fill
groups.why: Short-to-medium-lived trees that fill open gaps immediately, creating conditions for slower long-lived species beneath.
groups.mfNote: For Management-First: pioneers arrive naturally — aspen via clonal root sprouting, gray birch via seed, cherry after management. Skip purchasing.

species.name: Bigtooth Aspen
species.note: 318 Lepidoptera species. Clonal spread. Best for large open gaps.

species.name: Tulip Poplar
species.note: Fastest-growing native (3 ft/yr); 100+ ft mature height. South-facing, sheltered, lower slopes only at 2,000ft — northern fringe.

species.name: Black Cherry
species.note: ⚠️ Black knot fungus present on many properties. Survey existing trees before ordering.

species.name: Gray Birch
species.note: Short-lived pioneer (~40 yrs) for disturbed ground.

species.name: Sassafras
species.note: Aromatic pioneer with three distinct leaf shapes. Tiger and spicebush swallowtail host. Dioecious — plant both sexes for fruit. Clonal.

groups.name: Conifers
groups.why: Year-round structure and winter wildlife cover. Each species has a specific microsite. No natural seed source for spruce or fir at 2,000ft — these must be planted.

species.name: Balsam Fir
species.note: Best climate-risk profile. Dense winter cover. Cool ravines + north slopes.

species.name: Eastern White Pine
species.note: ⚠️ White Pine Needle Disease epidemic on Pocono Plateau — plant low-density only, never monoculture; ridge tops with good airflow. Wire cage.

species.name: Red Spruce
species.note: North-facing slopes ONLY — lower edge of climate range at 2,000ft. Wire cage.

species.name: Eastern Hemlock
species.note: ⚠️ Requires HWA treatment plan (soil drench every 4–6 yrs). Ravines only.

species.name: Pitch Pine
species.note: Most fire- and drought-resistant pine in the northeast. Re-sprouts from base after fire. Dry rocky ridge tops where other conifers fail.

groups.name: Understory / Mid-Story
groups.why: The mid-layer that provides spring flowers, summer berries, nesting cover, and four-season structure. A 2025 study found diverse understory increases total forest biomass by 50%+.

species.name: Allegheny Serviceberry
species.note: Management-First: arrives within 3–7 yrs of deer management. Save budget for hickory and conifers.

species.name: Pagoda Dogwood
species.note: Disease-free forest-interior dogwood. 40+ bird species. Spring Azure butterfly host.

species.name: Hop-Hornbeam
species.note: Management-First: arrives with deer management. Stump-sprouts. Skip purchasing.

species.name: Striped Maple
species.note: Northern hardwood companion at this exact elevation. White-striped green bark year-round. ⚠️ Deer-favored — wire cage essential.

species.name: Witch Hazel
species.note: Last forest bloomer (Oct–Nov yellow flowers). Seed pods explode 30ft. Outstanding four-season interest.

species.name: American Hornbeam
species.note: Sinewy musclewood bark. Deep-shade establisher near drainages and moist hollows. Disease-free.

groups.name: Fruiting / Flowering
groups.why: Trees selected for wildlife food and visual interest. Keep at ~8–10% so fruiting does not dominate the ecological restoration.
groups.mfNote: For Management-First: skip fruiting trees — budget goes to hickory and conifers first. Add in later years.

species.name: Native Sweet Crabapple
species.note: Rose-scented May bloom. 287 Lepidoptera species. Winter bird food.

species.name: American Chestnut (TACF)
species.note: Backcross hybrid. Contact PA-NJ TACF chapter (patacf.org) for seedlings.

species.name: American Plum
species.note: ⚠️ Same genus as black cherry — monitor for black knot. Bears, foxes, turkeys, birds. Forest edges only.

species.name: Black Walnut
species.note: ⚠️ Moist fertile coves only — allelopathic; suppresses nearby plants. 1–3 per cluster max. Most valuable native nut tree.

species.name: Fire-Resistant Pear
species.note: Moonglow, Harrow Sweet, or Luscious. DJ-confirmed at this elevation. Absorbs if Chestnut stock limited.

groups.name: Notable Specialty
groups.why: Ecologically native species rarely planted in restoration — each fills a specific microhabitat with high individual value.

species.name: Cucumber Magnolia
species.note: Native to Allegheny Plateau. Associates with yellow birch, hemlock, sugar maple. Almost never planted.

species.name: American Mountain Ash
species.note: Native to your exact elevation. Outstanding migrant bird food. Wire cage.

species.name: Black Gum
species.note: USFS climate winner. Best fall color of any eastern native. Absorbs overflow.

shrubs.name: American Hazelnut
shrubs.note: Hard mast for turkey + grouse. Polyphemus moth host.

shrubs.name: Nannyberry
shrubs.note: Winter berries for waxwings + robins. Cold-hardy throughout NEPA.

shrubs.name: Pennsylvania Hawthorn
shrubs.note: 159 Lepidoptera species. Best nesting cover available. Thorns are natural deer protection.

shrubs.name: Spicebush
shrubs.note: Spicebush Swallowtail butterfly host. Shade tolerant — true forest interior.

shrubs.name: Red Elderberry
shrubs.note: Native at your elevation. June berries for migrating thrushes.

shrubs.name: Highbush Blueberry
shrubs.note: Native to your pH 4–5.5 acidic upland soils. 100+ bird species consume berries. ⚠️ Wire cage — deer browse heavily.

shrubs.name: Gray Dogwood
shrubs.note: Disease-resistant. Wet edges + disturbed areas. Spreads by suckers — dense nesting cover. 40+ bird species.

shrubs.name: American Elderberry
shrubs.note: Distinct from red elderberry. Heavy July purple-black berries — critical for 40+ bird species during fall migration. Moist edges + margins.

shrubs.name: Ninebark
shrubs.note: Most adaptable native shrub. Steep banks, rocky outcrops, disturbed edges. Tolerates wet/dry/compacted/rocky.

shrubs.name: Arrowwood Viburnum
shrubs.note: Most adaptable native viburnum. Absorbs overflow.
